quote: 760. Is that the case for you? Sorry. 670. My apologies.
stewart_read: Jake states the credit threshold as 760, catches himself, and corrects to 670. The codex threshold is 670; a prior call (SESSION18_fcbf33c1) recorded a rep stating 650. This is the third data point on threshold drift. More critically, the affirmations at [02:45] — 'Yes. Yeah. Yes. Okay. Okay. Cool. Cool. Cool.' — appear to be Jake's voice, not Joseph's. If that read is correct, Joseph never verbally confirmed he meets the credit threshold. The closer may be walking into a proposal with an unverified qualifier.
alternative_hypothesis: The affirmations at [02:45] could be Joseph's — Deepgram speaker diarization on short overlapping utterances is unreliable. If Joseph did confirm, the only gap is the threshold error itself.
coaching_implication: Two separate coaching beats here: (1) Jake must know the correct threshold cold — 670, not 760. Run a quick drill. (2) Flag for the closer to verify credit eligibility explicitly at the start of the appointment before proceeding to proposal. Manager should also surface the 760/670/650 drift pattern to Kenny for a floor-wide threshold clarification.

stewart_read: Joseph asks 'Can I verify?' and receives no response — Jake's next line at [01:51] is 'How how long have you lived in, this house for?' The question is completely unaddressed. It is unclear what Joseph wanted to verify: the company's identity, the address Jake read back, or something else. This is an unresolved customer utterance that leaves a trust gap entering the appointment.
alternative_hypothesis: Joseph may have been asking to verify his own bill amount ('Can I verify?' as in 'let me check') rather than verifying Ion's identity. Either way, Jake did not respond to it.
coaching_implication: Coach Jake to treat any ambiguous customer question as a trust signal requiring acknowledgment before moving on. Suggested move: 'Of course — what would you like to verify?' costs two seconds and closes a potential suspicion loop. Flag in the handoff brief so the closer can open with a verification check.

quote: Oh, I'm like, I just moved to the this house there now. Oh, you did? The first month.
stewart_read: Joseph is a brand-new homeowner — moved in December, first month of bills. This is an enthusiasm signal, not a disqualifier. New homeowners are setting up their home's cost structure from scratch; the timing to lock in energy costs before Dominion's rates compound over years is a genuine value anchor. Jake hears it as a potential complication ('Oh, you did?') and moves past it without building on it.
alternative_hypothesis: Jake may have read the new-homeowner status as a bill-data gap (only one month of history) and deprioritized the anchor to keep the call moving. That is a reactive read — the enthusiasm signal was available regardless of bill history.
coaching_implication: Show Jake how to flip the new-homeowner signal into a forward-looking anchor. The specific bridge language should come from Kenny's coaching sessions or Ion-designated exemplar calls — do not invent numbers or savings claims. The coaching beat is: new homeowner = prime timing, not thin data.

quote: We do have a couple different, like, kinda, like, pricing or financing options.
stewart_read: Jake volunteers that Ion has 'a couple different pricing or financing options' without being asked. This is mild scope creep — the setter's job is to qualify and schedule, not to preview the closer's product menu. It does not derail the call, but it opens a door to pricing questions Jake is not equipped to answer at setter stage.
alternative_hypothesis: Jake may have introduced financing options as a soft qualifier setup (leading into the credit check), which is a legitimate structural move. The framing is loose enough that it reads as accidental rather than strategic.
coaching_implication: Coach Jake to lead the credit qualifier with the program framing from the codex template ('We have a solar program that saves you money immediately') rather than previewing financing options. The options preview invites closer-tier questions the setter cannot answer.

quote: come over and and visit you and kinda show you that design and and how it works and answer any questions that you have, just about it
stewart_read: Jake books an in-person visit ('come over and visit you') without completing any of the required button-up protocol elements: no 'at your computer' instruction, no 'not driving' anchor, no one-hour block stated with intent, no 'is that fair' verbal handshake, no text follow-up promise. The appointment is set but structurally bare. Additionally, the call is framed as an in-person visit — the codex specifies a phone appointment. If this is intended as an in-home consultation rather than a phone closer call, that is a structural difference the handoff brief must flag.
alternative_hypothesis: Ion may run in-home consultations as the standard closer format in this market, in which case the 'come over' framing is correct procedure. The codex specifies phone appointments, but Kenny should confirm whether in-home is the norm for this rep's territory.
coaching_implication: Regardless of in-person vs phone format, the button-up checklist is incomplete. Coach Jake to run the full confirmation sequence: time block, computer/location anchor, 'is that fair' handshake, text promise. Each skipped element raises no-show risk. Confirm with Kenny whether in-home visits are the standard format for this market.

quote: In order to qualify for all of them, there's two things you need. One is to be on the title of your house, which I assume is no issue for you.
stewart_read: Jake assumes Joseph is the sole title holder ('which I assume is no issue for you') without asking. The codex requires confirming who is on the title — not assuming. If a spouse or co-owner is on the title, they must be present at the appointment. Jake skips this entirely, leaving the appointment vulnerable to a no-show or a 'I need to talk to my wife' deflection at the closer stage.
alternative_hypothesis: Joseph called back on his own, which might suggest he is the primary decision-maker. But assumption is not confirmation — the codex is explicit that this must be asked, not inferred.
coaching_implication: This is a protocol gap, not a judgment call. Coach Jake to ask 'Is anyone else on the title with you?' before moving to the credit qualifier. If a co-owner exists, the appointment must include them. Manager should replay this moment and have Jake practice the title question as a natural conversational beat, not a checkbox.

quote: I have you blocked out for an hour just so you you you don't feel rushed, but, really, it just kinda depends on how many questions you have. So usually, it takes about half that time if if even that.
stewart_read: Jake states the one-hour block (a required button-up element) but immediately undercuts it by saying 'it just kinda depends' and 'usually takes about half that time if even that.' The codex intent of the one-hour block is to signal that the appointment is substantive and worth the customer's full attention. Hedging it down to 'half that time if even that' reduces the perceived weight of the appointment and may lower Joseph's preparation investment.
alternative_hypothesis: Jake may be trying to reduce Joseph's time anxiety to prevent a cancellation. That is a legitimate intent — but the execution trades appointment gravity for short-term reassurance.
coaching_implication: Coach Jake to state the one-hour block with confidence and leave it there. The reassurance move ('usually takes half that time') is available if Joseph pushes back on time, but volunteering it unprompted softens the appointment's perceived importance. The codex framing — 'I blocked off that time so you don't feel rushed' — is the right stopping point.

quote: Hey, Joseph. What's up, man? How can I help you?
stewart_read: Jake's opening after confirming Joseph's name is 'What's up, man? How can I help you?' — which inverts the call's purpose. Joseph called back on a message; Jake should be the one explaining why Ion reached out and establishing legitimacy, not asking Joseph what he needs. The codex intro template establishes Ion's identity and the Tesla-certified framing in the first 30 seconds. Jake skips this entirely, leaving Joseph to explain his own call back ('I think I've made a call from someone someone called Kenny').
alternative_hypothesis: Because Joseph called in rather than being cold-called, Jake may have reasonably treated this as an inbound service call where the customer leads. But the legitimacy gap is real regardless — Joseph does not know what Ion is or why he should trust them.
coaching_implication: Coach Jake to pivot from 'how can I help you' to a brief legitimacy frame as soon as he confirms Joseph's identity: who Ion is, why they reached out, and what the call is about. The Tesla-certified framing from the codex intro is the right tool here. Thirty seconds of legitimacy work at the top reduces the trust gap the entire call has to carry.
